topic: Two siblings inherit their grandmother's house
raw_output:
[story 1]

The key felt cold and heavy in my palm, a tangible weight of expectation. When my aunt finally dragged me to the crumbling manor, the air inside immediately hit me—thick with the smell of mildew, stale potpourri, and something vaguely metallic. I stood in the grand foyer, my shoulders tensing beneath my borrowed sweater. Everything was draped in faded velvet, the furniture sagging like weary old men. I kept my gaze fixed on the peeling wallpaper, tracing the pattern with my eyes as if searching for an escape route. "It's beautiful, dear," Aunt Clara chirped, her voice too bright for the oppressive silence. I managed a tight nod, my jaw clenched so hard I could feel the ache in my molars. I moved toward the library, my steps deliberate and slow, each footfall echoing unnervingly on the warped floorboards. I just wanted to be anywhere else, anywhere clean.

[story 2]

I surveyed the drawing-room, a cavern of dust motes dancing in the weak afternoon light filtering through grimy panes. The sheer volume of forgotten things overwhelmed me. Tarnished silver, moth-eaten tapestries, stacks of yellowed correspondence—it all seemed to press in, suffocating the space. I pulled my cardigan tighter around myself, a reflexive gesture against the pervasive chill that seemed to emanate from the very foundations of the house. "We should start sorting things," my brother suggested, his tone annoyingly cheerful. I didn't look up from the hideous porcelain figurine on the mantelpiece. My hands were shaking slightly as I reached for a heavy, dusty volume, the texture of the brittle paper grating against my fingertips. I felt a profound urge to simply walk out the front door and never look back at the suffocating grandeur within.

[story 3]

The attic was worse than I imagined. It was a landscape of forgotten junk, shrouded in cobwebs thick as fishing nets. A stale, musty odor clung to my clothes, a heavy blanket I couldn't shake off. I crouched beside a trunk overflowing with brittle lace and yellowed photographs, my breath catching in shallow, quick bursts. Every object seemed to hold a layer of neglect, a testament to years of undisturbed decay. I pushed a stray strand of hair from my face, the movement feeling labored. "It’s full of memories," my sister whispered from the doorway, her voice laced with forced enthusiasm. I just stared at the gloom, my stomach tightening into a knot of unpleasant tension. I wanted to smash something, anything, just to break the oppressive stillness.
